coredata.dc:title: A simple method to achieve a directional and resonant random lasing emission using graphene quantum dots as scattering elements
coredata.dc:creator: Ghasempour Ardakani, Abbas
Shahvandpour, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a simple and cost-effective method to obtain directionality and enhancement of intensity in random lasers (RLs). For this purpose, a thin liquid dye layer containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is sandwiched between two microscope glass slides. GQDs provide optical feedback for resonant RLemission via multiple scattering. The resonant RLemission from GQDs doped dye solution is coupled into microscope glass slides providing a waveguide structure. Two bright spots are observed along edge sides of microscope glass slides. Resonant RL emission also occurs in front side of the microscope glass slides which is not directional and significantly weaker than the emission in the edge side. Furthermore, it is demonstrated when a dye solution without any scattering particles is sandwiched between two microscope glass slides, two emission spots with high intensity are seen along the edge side whose spectrum includes a broad and intensive single peak.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Random lasers
Graphene quantum dots
Directionality

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesized GQDs, (b) TEM image of synthesized GQDs, (c) XRD pattern for synthesized GQDs.

(a), (b) A double sided tape is stuck to a microscope glass slide; (c) Several droplets of dye solution containing GQDs with certain volume are deposited in the middle of double sided tape; (d) The solution is distributed uniformly in the empty space at the middle of the double sided tape; (d), (f) Finally, another microscope slide is placed on the double sided tape and by pressing of it the RL cell is obtained.
A schematic of experimental setup used for measuring the RL emission properties.
Emission spectrum of GQDs based RL recorded in the front side of sample at pump energy of 6.03 mJ/pulse.
Emission spectrum of GQDs based RL recorded in the edge side of sample at pump energy of 6.03 mJ/pulse.
(a) A photograph of directional RL emission at a pump energy above threshold, (b) Simultaneous presentation of emission spectra recorded in the front and edge sides.
Integrated emitted intensity as a function of pump energy for the RL emission radiated in front and edge sides.

Normalized integrated emitted intensity (NIEI) versus the angle which makes the optical fiber of spectrometer with direction at which maximum integrated intensity is obtained at both front and edge sides.
A schematic for illustration of mechanism leading to directionality of the proposed RL.
A photograph of directional emission observed in the edge side of cell containing dye solution without any graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: A simple method to achieve a directional and resonant random lasing emission using graphene quantum dots as scattering elements

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a simple and cost-effective method to obtain directionality and enhancement of intensity in random lasers (RLs). For this purpose, a thin liquid dye layer containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is sandwiched between two microscope glass slides. GQDs provide optical feedback for resonant RLemission via multiple scattering. The resonant RLemission from GQDs doped dye solution is coupled into microscope glass slides providing a waveguide structure. Two bright spots are observed along edge sides of microscope glass slides. Resonant RL emission also occurs in front side of the microscope glass slides which is not directional and significantly weaker than the emission in the edge side. Furthermore, it is demonstrated when a dye solution without any scattering particles is sandwiched between two microscope glass slides, two emission spots with high intensity are seen along the edge side whose spectrum includes a broad and intensive single peak.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most interesting human innovations has been the introduction of random lasers (RLs). RLs are kind of lasers that, like conventional and well-known lasers, have three basic components of lasers, with the difference that the feedback mechanism in them is provided by the scattering process. Depending on whether the scattered light can return to its original position after scattering, RLs are divided into resonant and non-resonant RLs [1,2]. In non-resonant RLs, the disorder degree of scattering particles inside the gain medium is weak, so the light scattered by any particles never returns to its original position, and therefore interference effects do not occur for the light waves. In the emission spectrum of these RLs, there are no separate resonant peaks resulting from interference effects [1]. The first study in this field dates back to 1968, this year Letokhov theoretically proposed the generation of laser like emission from an active medium filled with scatterers [3].

In 1998, Cao introduced resonant RLs in which the disorder degree in random systems is very strong and therefore the light wave can return to its original position after several scattering events and form a closed loop, thus providing the possibility of interference for the light wave [4]. Each of these closed loops can be called a random laser cavity. In the emission spectrum of this type of laser, several resonant peaks appear at the same resonant frequencies corresponding to random cavities [1,5].

The special radiation characteristics of RLs, such as the lack of directional radiation and the low spatial coherence of these lasers, along with their small size, simple design and low cost, have given them many applications [2,6,7].

One of the detrimental properties of RLs is their nondirectional emission which can limit their industrial applications. So far different attempts have been made to overcome this problem. Random fiber lasers (RFL) are a very smart mechanism for achieving directional radiation from RLs [8–10]. Because complex conditions are required to fabricate random fiber lasers, other simpler ways have been suggested to obtain directionality in RLs. In 2006, for a RL in the regime of weak scattering, it was found that it is possible to control the frequency and direction of laser radiation by changing the geometry of pumping, the scattering mean free path and absorption length [11]. In 2009, by optical excitation of an organic-inorganic grating in the presence of irregularities, the authors obtained a RL with directional emission which was due to the diffraction resulting from the grating [12]. In 2011, a RLwas developed based on a plasmon waveguide. The plasmon waveguide included a random coating of gold nanoisland with a layer of dye-doped polymer. The designed RL in this work had a low threshold and narrow band. The main features of this RL were the overlap of plasmonic resonance, the photoluminescence spectrum and the confinement created by the waveguide layer. It is worth mentioning that the feedback mechanism used in this research, i.e. the waveguide-plasmonic scattering mechanism, is able to create directional outputs from RLs [13]. By placing spatial light modulator in front of the pump source, the spatial pump profile can be optimized to achieve directional radiation [14]. Shen et al. used oxygen plasma to create wrinkles with random spaces on the surface of a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) slab [15]. Then they placed a thin dye layer between the wrinkled PDMS slab and a flat one. By optical pumping the sample, a unidirectional RL emission was observed at the edge of PDMS chip. This structure showed low threshold intensity and its emission was sensitive to the position of pumping beam with respect to sample surface. Zhang et al. proposed and fabricated a directional RL by sandwiching a dye solution containing silver nanoparticles between two MgF2 plane plates [16]. When this RL was pumped, sharp peaks appeared in the emission spectrum. Because of forming waveguide structure in this RL system, the lasing emission was unidirectional and had low threshold.

Here we report the fabrication of a resonant RL with directional emission from graphene quantum dots (GQDs) acting as scattering centers. GQDs are a group of quantum dots which have attracted a lot of attention due to their excellent photostability, small size, biocompatibility, highly tunable photoluminescence property, chemical inertness and so on [17]. QDs are zero dimensional nanostructures in which electrons are confined in three dimensions. They exist in different forms such as clusters, nanoparticles, and semiconductor nanocrystals. QDs are well known as artificial atoms because of their discrete electronic states similar to atoms and molecules. In QDs the energy band gap increases with the decrease of their size due to quantum size effect. Furthermore, QDs are unique because their optoelectronic properties can be tuned by changing their size and shape. Special properties of QDs make it possible their using RLs [18–20].

A single layer separated from graphite is called graphene which is one of the carbon allotropes. In the graphene layer, each carbon atom is covalently bonded to three other carbon atoms. The angle between the covalent bonds is approximately 120°. Graphene is a semi-metal with a zero band gap which can be composited with various materials, including organic materials, polymers, and semiconductor nanoparticles. Interesting features of graphene structures have led researchers to use them in the field of RLs. In 2012, it was reported that the performance of a RL made of ZnO nanorods could be improved by adding reduced graphene oxide (RGO) nanoflakes. Increasing the emission intensity, decreasing the threshold and FWHM of the laser peaks were the results of adding RGO nanoflakes in this RL. The reason for the improvement in performance was the effect of surface plasmon resonance in RGO nanoflakes [21]. In 2013, a comparison between GQDs and carbon dots (CD) showed that due to the geometrical advantages in the structure of GQDs, they are more suitable for use as gain medium in RLs. For example, a RL made of GQDs had a lower threshold than CDs [22]. In 2018, a multicolor RL was made from a combination of semiconductor nanoparticles and vertical-graphene nanowalls (GNWs) network. The most notable feature of this RL was its low laser threshold (threshold energy density ≈10 nJ cm −2
) due to the excellent ability of GNWs to trap photons. It was also observed that by coating Ag/SiO2 on the GNWs, the threshold pump fluence reaches about 1 nJ cm −2 . The reason for this significant decrease in the threshold was the combined ability to trap photons and increase of plasmonic power in this case. As mentioned, one of the most prominent radiant features of RLs is the lack of directional radiation, which, of course, has led to applications for RLs, for example in screens [23].

In our previous work, we have used GQDs as scattering elements in rhodamine B dye solution based RL [24]. We have compared the emission spectrum from the fabricated GQDs based RL with a TiO2 based RL. We found that applying GQDs in RLs causes their emission spectrum to become multimode. Furthermore, GQDs based RLs have longer operating lifetime compared to ones based on TiO2. Therefore, our results in Ref. [24] confirm that random lasing emission from GQDs based RLs is in the resonant regime. In addition, we have demonstrated that random lasing threshold decreases with increasing the concentration of GQDs scattering particles [24] and emission spectrum changes with varying the excitation position on the sample. Here, we indicate that both directional and multi-peak emissions can be obtained from GQDs based RLs. Here, we apply a simple and low cost structure to achieve directionality in RLs. Two microscope slides are used for preparing the RL cell to hold suspension of GQDs in dye solution. The waveguide properties of the microscope slide enable us to observe directional RL emission from the edge side. The structure proposed in this paper is simpler and more cost effective than ones in previous works mentioned above.

The remaining parts of the paper are organized as follows. The details for sample preparation and experimental setup are given in section 2. We present our experimental results and some discussion in section 3. Finally, we finished the paper with some conclusion in section 4.

We use pyrolysis of citric acid purchased (99.5%) from Merk to grow GQDs according to Ref. [25]. 9 g of citric acid was melted in a beaker through a hot plate up to 120 °C. Citric acid starts melting at 60 °C and the whole powder melts at 108 °C. After about 4 min, the white color of the liquid changes to yellow. Finally, after 15 min at 120 °C an orange and viscous liquid was obtained. Now, the synthesis is finished and beaker was removed from the hot plate. Then we allow the beaker to cool down and its temperature reached to 60 °C. A photograph image of the synthesized GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The TEM image of synthesized GQDs is displayed in Fig. 1(b). One can see from this figure that the average diameter of GQDs is about 5 nm. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of the synthesized GQDs is displayed in Fig. 1(c) where we see a broad peak around 2θ = 22° similar to graphite lattice spacing due to highly disordered carbon atoms [26].

To obtain the gain medium, rhodamin B dye (sigma Aldrich) was dissolved in ethyleneglycol (Merck, 99.5% purity) with concentration of 8.77 mM and then the solution was stirred by a magnetic stirrer for 40 min. Then 2 ml of the prepared gain solution is added into the beaker containing GQDs achieved in the previous step and stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 2 h to obtain a homogenous solution. It should be noted that gentle heating is necessary during the stirring process to prevent the solution hardening.

We choose a double-sided tape with thickness of 50 μm and cut it into rectangular pieces such that covers the whole of a microscope slide with dimension of 7.5 × 2.5 × 0.1 cm. Now, we cut a strip with dimension of 5.5 × 1.5 cm in the middle of the rectangular piece obtained in the previous stage. Then we stick the obtained tape having an empty space in its middle to a microscope slide such that covers the whole of its area. Afterward, we transferred several droplets of dye gain solution to the central region and then place another microscope slide on the first one such that sticks to the double sided tape. Fig. 2 shows the process of preparing the directional and resonant GQDs RL sample.

A schematic of experimental set up is shown in Fig. 3 . The second harmonic of a Nd: YAG pulsed laser with pulse duration of 10 ns and repetition rate of 10 Hz is applied for pumping the RL sample. The pump light passes through an aperture diaphragm and then is normally focused on the sample by a cylindrical lens with a focal length of 15 cm. The spot size of pump light on the sample is about 10.3 mm × 0.35 mm. The emission spectrum of RL was collected from both front and edge sides by an optical fiber coupled to a spectrometer (ocean optics, 0.1 nm resolution). Finally, the emission spectra were recorded by a computer connected to the spectrometer.

In our previous work, we have described the detail for fabrication of resonant RL based on GQDs as scattering elements [24]. We have shown that number of lasing peaks and their emission intensity increased with increase of GQDs concentration. Furthermore, the RL threshold decreased as a consequence of concentration increasing. In Ref. [24], we have compared the photo-stability of our GQDs based RL with a TiO2 based RL and have shown that a stronger photostability occurs for a RL consisting of GQDs. Here, we aim to obtain a directional random lasing emission from the fabricated RL in our previous work. For this purpose, we place the prepared sample in section 2.1 in the experimental setup illustrated in Fig. 3. We set the pumping energy at 6.03 mJ per pulse and record the emission spectrum in front of sample at an angle of 38.89° with respect to surface normal of the sample. The distance between optical fiber and sample is to be 8 cm. The corresponding emission spectrum is shown in Fig. 4 . One can see some subnanometer peaks in the spectrum that confirms the existence of resonant feedback in the GQDs based RL. However, there is no sign of directionality for this laser emission detected in the front side of sample. The optical feedback in this RL system provides by multiple scattering of light due to the presence of GQDs in the dye solution. As shown in our previous work [24], when we pump the dye solution without any GQDs at the same intensity as in Fig. 4, only a broad emission peak with a very low intensity appears in the emission spectrum due to the spontaneous emission. Therefore, the presence of GQDs is necessary for emergence of sharp lasing peaks in Fig. 4. The position of peaks in Fig. 4 is determined by the resonance frequency of the random cavities or closed paths formed as a result of multiple scattering in the disordered medium. Random cavities in which the lasing condition is satisfied can participate in the random lasing action.

Now, we change the position of the fiber and placed it in the edge side of sample at a distance of 17 cm from it. We record again the emission spectrum and show the corresponding results in Fig. 5 . As shown in this figure, narrow peaks emerge again in the emission spectrum. But, spectrum obtained in the edge side of sample is significantly stronger than that obtained in the front side.

In addition, a type of directionality is observed in the laser emission from edge side unlike front side. We display a photograph of laser emission on a screen placed at distance of 14 cm from sample edge in Fig. 6 (a). In this figure, one clearly sees two bright spots. But, we do not observe such spots for the RL emission in the front side of sample. For more comparison, the emission spectrum measured in edge and front sides are also given simultaneously in Fig. 6 (b).

It is clearly seen that the directional output emitted intensity in the edge side is much stronger than nondirectional emission observed in the front side. The output emissions of RLs do not generally show any directionality. However, our proposed RL in this paper shows a kind of directionality in its edge side in opposite to its front side. In fact, the structure and shape of the applied cell leads to this directionality. In the RL emission observed in both edge and front sides, optical feedback is achieved from multiple scattering of light by GQDs, while optical gain is provided by the dye solution. RL emission can occur in every place of the system provided the lasing condition is met. The microscope glass slides provide a path for propagation of RL emission in a direction parallel to their surface. Therefore, we see two RL spots along the edge side of microscope glass slides. In the following we will discuss the mechanism which is responsible for directionality of RL emission in our structure. Due to simplicity in fabrication and operation the proposed directional RL in this paper can facile practical applications of RLs.

Afterward, in Fig. 7 we plot the integrated intensity as a function of pump energies for laser emission radiated from both front and edge sides of sample. It is clearly seen in two curves that after a specific threshold pump energy the increase of integrated intensity with the pump energy occurs in a steeper slope. This threshold behavior is one of main features used to confirm lasing action. As shown in Fig. 7, both curves display the threshold behavior. However, the lasing emission in the edge side demonstrates a larger slope efficiency. Because the optical feedback in the proposed RL results from the multiple scattering of light by GQDs, the microscope glass slides play no significant role in the optical feedback. In other words, the strength of optical feedback for RL emission in both edge and front sides are almost the same. So we expect that the laser thresholds for the radiations observed in front and edge sides are the same. But, the RL emission can couple from any points of the pumped region to the microscope glass slides and propagates through them. So, we see a stronger emission and larger slope efficiency for the emission in the edge side.

To quantify the amount of directionality of fabricated RL, we measure the angular distribution of the output emission intensity in both front and edge sides. To do this, the emission spectra at different angles with enough fine steps are recorded and finally the integrated intensity is plotted in Fig. 8 as a function of angle which optical fiber of spectrometer makes with the direction at which maximum integrated intensity is achieved. It should be noted that the integrated intensity at different angles is normalized to one at angle of 0°. Thus, the angles are computed from the line at which maximum integrated emitted intensity is obtained in both front and edge sides. We define the FWHM in Fig. 8 as the divergence angle. One can see that the divergence angle of the lasing beam in the edge side is 2.33° while in the front side the laser beam has a divergence angle of 10.65°. Therefore, strong directionality is obtained for the RL beam in the edge side compared to one in the front side.

Now we discuss about the mechanism for directionality of RL output in the edge side. Because the refractive index of microscope glass slide (1.5) is larger than refractive index of air and ethylene glycol (1.43), we expect that two microscope glass slides used for preparing the RL cell act as two slab waveguides. When dye solution containing GQDs is pumped, the resonant RL emission happens in all directions. This nondirectional RL emission can be coupled into two glass slides and propagates through them. So, we observe two separate laser spots along the cell of RL as shown in Fig. 6 (a). We display a schematic diagram to illustrate the mechanism leading to the directionality of RL emission in Fig. 9 . It should be noted that the coupling of RL emission to the microscope glass slides can occur for every points of gain medium.

To more understand the role of microscope glass slides in the directionality of RL, we insert a dye solution without GQDs in the prepared cell above and placed it in the setup displayed in Fig. 3. We record the emission spectrum at both front and edge sides for a pump energy of 4.14 mJ and show the corresponding results in Fig. 10 (a). The distance between the screen and edge of cell is about 14 cm. One can see that there is only one peak in the emission spectrum with FWHM of 28 nm. The intensity of emission at the edge side is significantly higher than one at the front side. Furthermore, the emission at edge side consists of two bright spots unlike the emission at front side as shown in Fig. 10 (b). Therefore, the cell consisting of two microscope slides provides a way to obtain a directional radiation from the florescent materials without using any complicated and expensive elements.

Consequently, the strategy introduced here for cell construction not only makes it possible the directional output emission from RLs but also from other florescent dye solutions. We envision that this structure can be a promising platform for potential applications of RLs and other dye solution in the absence of scattering particles. It should be noted that in random fiber lasers that are good platform to obtain directionality in RLs, long SMFs are required to provide enough multiple scattering (40 km length). Furthermore, introduction of FBGs in fibers to achieve directionality in random RLs needs complicated and expensive equipments. Therefore, waveguide structures mentioned above are easier to fabricate than random fiber lasers to get directional lasing emission. In addition, the waveguide structure described in this paper has less complication and lower cost compared to the previous waveguide structures to produce directional emission in RLs.

We have demonstrated a simple and low cost resonant directional RL by sandwiching a rhodamine B dye solution containing GQDs between two microscope glass slides. Our results confirm that the lasing emission along the edge side consists of two spots whose spectrum includes a number of resonance peaks with narrow linewidth. However, no directionality is observed from the output emission in the front side. In addition, such directionality takes place for the florescent emission of dye solution without scattering particles sandwiched between two microscope glass slides. Consequently, the suggested structure in this paper can be a promising tool for directionality of RLs and florescent dye solutions as well as enhancement of their emission intensity.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
A. G. A conceived the idea. M. S and A. G. A did the experiments and measurements. A. G. A and M. S wrote the paper. A. G. A revised the paper along the reviewers' comments.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rafieipour, A. Ghasempour Ardakani, and F. Daneshmand, "Resonant Random Laser Emission from Graphene Quantum Dots Doped Dye Solution," Laser Physics 30(11), 367-374 (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonizatin degree of citric acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, J. Shao, C. Chen, H. Li, R. Wang, Y. Chi, X. Lin, and G. Chen,"Blue Luminescent Graphene Quantum Dots and Graphene Oxide Prepared by Tuning the Carbonizatin Degree of Citric Acid," Carbon 50(12), 4738-4743 (2012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. L. Wu, M. X. Gao, T. T. Wang, X. Y. Wan, L. L. Zheng, C. Z. Huang, "A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots," Nanoscale, 6(7), 3868-3874 (2014).
